title: Cosmology: Creation and the Universe
subtitle: Islamic and Biblical Perspectives on the Origin, Structure, and Expansion of Creation
summary: Both Islamic and Biblical traditions contain cosmological teachings about the origin and nature of the universe. Modern scientific cosmology—based on observations of cosmic expansion, the Big Bang hypothesis, and the universe's age—intersects with religious creation narratives in ways that generate both scholarly engagement and popular enthusiasm. This article examines these perspectives without conflating theology with physics.

seo.metaTitle: Cosmology in Islam and Christianity: Creation, Expansion, and Scientific Evidence
seo.metaDescription: Explore Islamic and Biblical cosmological teachings on creation, the structure of the heavens, and cosmic expansion—and how they relate to modern scientific understanding.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.body: Both Islam and Christianity have rich cosmological traditions. The Islamic scriptures describe the creation of the heavens and earth in detailed language, while the Judeo-Christian Bible opens with a systematic creation account. In the modern era, scientific cosmology—the study of the universe's structure, origin, and evolution—has developed a comprehensive model based on observations and mathematical physics. The relationship between these religious narratives and scientific findings has been a subject of scholarly analysis, popular debate, and occasionally, overclaimed parallels. This article presents the religious teachings, the scientific model, and examines them on their own terms without imposing one framework onto another.

sections.title: Islamic Cosmology: The Quranic Account
sections.body:
The Quran provides a cosmological narrative centered on God's creative act and the structure of the cosmos. The most theologically significant verse for comparative cosmology discussions is Surah Al-Anbiyaa 21:30, which describes the primordial state of creation: "Have those who disbelieved not considered that the heavens and the earth were a joined entity, and We separated them and made from water every living thing? Then will they not believe?" This verse uses the Arabic terms 'ratq' (joined/fused) and 'fatq' (separated/split), describing a process of differentiation from an undifferentiated state.

The Quranic cosmology presents creation as an ordered process. In Surah Fussilat 41:11, the heavens are described as emerging from smoke (dukhan): "Then He directed Himself to the heaven while it was smoke and said to it and to the earth, "Come [into being], willingly or by compulsion." They said, "We have come willingly."" This imagery conveys a primordial state of matter preceding the formation of structured heavens and earth.

The structure of creation is presented in stages. Surah Al-Baqara 2:29 describes God creating all things on earth, then directing Himself to the heaven and making seven heavens: "It is He who created for you all of that which is on the earth. Then He directed Himself to the heaven, [His being above all creation], and made them seven heavens, and He is Knowing of all things." The timeframe is specified in Surah Fussilat 41:12: "And He completed them as seven heavens within two days and inspired in each heaven its command."

Crucially, the Quran emphasizes cosmic expansion. Surah Adh-Dhaariyat 51:47 states: "And the heaven We constructed with strength, and indeed, We are [its] expander." The translation and interpretation of 'musi'un' (the form of the Arabic root) has been subject to scholarly dispute. Some classical and modern translators render it as 'expanders' or 'extending,' while others, including Pickthall and Yusuf Ali, translate the root as 'possessing vast power' or 'ample means,' emphasizing divine omnipotence rather than spatial expansion of the cosmos. This represents a genuine, unresolved translation controversy among Islamic scholars. Contemporary interpreters who emphasize the expansion meaning note its resonance with modern cosmology; others maintain that the verse's primary theological message concerns divine power rather than physics. The verse has received particular attention in modern discussions of cosmology precisely because this translation ambiguity exists.

The Quran also describes the heavens using architectural imagery. Surah An-Naazi'aat 79:27-28 states: "Are you a more difficult creation or is the heaven? Allah constructed it. He raised its ceiling and proportioned it." The Arabic word rendered 'ceiling' here is samk (سمك), denoting a roof or raised structure. Like the Hebrew raqia' ('expanse' or 'firmament') discussed later in the Biblical cosmology section, this is the shared architectural vocabulary of ancient Near Eastern cosmology, in which the sky was widely conceived as a solid, dome-like covering or roof over a flat earth. Just as scholars caution against reading Genesis's dome-like sky as a scientifically precise description of atmospheric structure, the same caution applies to the Quran's 'ceiling' language: mainstream exegesis reads it as architectural metaphor conveying divine construction, order, and majesty, not as a technical claim about the physical composition of the sky. Treating one tradition's ancient cosmological idiom as merely 'poetic' while treating the other's as a literal scientific description would be inconsistent; both are best read within their shared ancient conceptual world.

sections.title: Quran 21:30 and the 'Big Bang Parallel' Debate
sections.body:
Among Muslim scholars and educated audiences in the 20th century, Surah 21:30's description of the heavens and earth as a 'joined entity' (ratq) that was then 'separated' (fatq) has been interpreted as potentially prefiguring the Big Bang cosmological model. In this reading, the verse's imagery of initial unity followed by cosmic differentiation resonates with modern physics' description of the universe expanding from an initial singularity.

This parallel has generated enthusiasm in some Muslim circles, appearing in popular works and educational contexts as evidence that the Quran contains scientific knowledge. However, Islamic scholars emphasize important distinctions. Classical Islamic exegetes (mufassirun) including Ibn Abbas, At-Tabari, and Al-Zamakhshari interpreted this verse within a theological framework: the point was not to predict physics but to demonstrate God's power to bring order from undifferentiated state, and to anchor the unity of creation in divine action.

Modern Muslim scholars offer nuanced readings. Some argue that the Quranic description is entirely compatible with a Big Bang model and may indeed have been pointing toward this truth. Others note that the Quranic language is theological, not scientific—it addresses meaning and divine agency, not mechanism—and that drawing direct equivalences risks both overreading the scripture and oversimplifying modern physics. Still others point out that the Quran does not discuss expansion, cooling, or particle physics; these are inferences we add when comparing to Big Bang theory.

The scholarly consensus is that while no contradiction exists between Quranic cosmology and Big Bang cosmology, the direct 'prefigurement' claim requires careful qualification. The Quran is not a science textbook; it is a revelation addressing theological and moral truths. That these may be compatible with accurate science is theologically meaningful but does not mean the Quran is making scientific predictions.

sections.title: Cosmic Expansion: Modern Physics and Quranic Language
sections.body:
Modern cosmology, established through observations by Edwin Hubble in the 1920s and refined through decades of astronomical data, confirms that the universe is expanding. Distant galaxies are receding from us, and the universe is growing larger over time. This expansion is described by Einstein's field equations and understood through the Big Bang model: the universe began in an extremely hot, dense state and has been expanding and cooling ever since. The universe is approximately 13.8 billion years old based on multiple independent lines of evidence.

The Quranic emphasis on divine expansion in Surah 51:47 has resonated with modern Muslim physicists and theologians. The verse's use of 'musi'un' (expandable, expanders) in reference to the heavens takes on particular significance in light of twentieth-century physics. This has led some scholars to describe the Quran as containing an 'early hint' at cosmic expansion.

However, scholars also note that Quranic language about expansion was historically understood in theological terms—God's creative power extends the heavens as an expression of divine majesty—rather than as a description of measurable cosmological recession. The verse does not quantify the expansion rate, describe its cause, or address the physics of how it occurs. From a methodological standpoint, finding confirmation of modern physics in ancient religious texts is legitimate and meaningful, but it remains distinct from the empirical discovery of those physics through observation and measurement.

The scientific model itself underwent significant revision in the 1990s with the discovery of cosmic acceleration, driven by dark energy. This unexpected finding further complicated any simple parallels between Quranic language and physics, reminding us that scientific models are dynamic and subject to revision based on evidence.

sections.title: Biblical Cosmology: Genesis and the Created Order
sections.body:
The Biblical account of creation is presented systematically in the opening chapters of Genesis. Genesis 1:1 begins with an absolute statement: "In the beginning, God created the heavens and the earth." This establishes creation as a divine act rather than an accidental or emergent process. The account then unfolds across six days of creation, each followed by divine approval ('It was good').

Genesis 1:2-5 describes the initial state: "The earth was formless and empty. Darkness was on the surface of the deep and God’s Spirit was hovering over the surface of the waters. God said, “Let there be light,” and there was light. God saw the light, and saw that it was good. God divided the light from the darkness. God called the light “day”, and the darkness he called “night”. There was evening and there was morning, the first day." This passage presents creation as proceeding from undifferentiation to order, with light (representing order and goodness) emerging from darkness and formlessness.

The heavenly bodies are created on the fourth day. Genesis 1:14-19 describes their purpose: "God said, “Let there be lights in the expanse of the sky to divide the day from the night; and let them be for signs to mark seasons, days, and years; and let them be for lights in the expanse of the sky to give light on the earth”; and it was so. God made the two great lights: the greater light to rule the day, and the lesser light to rule the night. He also made the stars. God set them in the expanse of the sky to give light to the earth, and to rule over the day and over the night, and to divide the light from the darkness. God saw that it was good. There was evening and there was morning, a fourth day." The New Testament develops this with declarations of cosmic creation. Colossians 1:16-17 attributes all creation to Christ: "For by him all things were created, in the heavens and on the earth, things visible and things invisible, whether thrones or dominions or principalities or powers; all things have been created through him, and for him. He is before all things, and in him all things are held together."

The Psalms present creation as revealing divine nature. Psalm 19:1 states: "The heavens declare the glory of God. The expanse shows his handiwork." This theological position—that creation itself testifies to the Creator—has been central to Christian natural theology throughout history.

sections.title: Biblical Cosmology and Modern Science
sections.body:
Christian scholars have engaged with modern cosmology in various ways. Some emphasize compatibility: the Biblical narrative of creation is not incompatible with a Big Bang model; both describe a universe that came into being through a transcendent creative act. Others note that the Biblical account was written in ancient Near Eastern cultural context and employs the cosmological model of that era (a fixed flat earth with a dome-like sky), which differs from modern understanding. Still others argue that such details are incidental to the theological core—that God created the universe—and that the 'how' is outside the scope of revelation.

The Hebrews 11:3 verse states: "By faith, we understand that the universe has been framed by the word of God, so that what is seen has not been made out of things which are visible." Some Christian theologians have interpreted this as compatible with quantum field theory and Big Bang cosmology—that the physical universe emerges from a non-material source or initial condition, consistent with the notion that all visible reality came from something beyond the merely material.

Christian scholarship has generally avoided claiming that Genesis 'predicted' modern physics. Instead, the focus has been on showing that no contradiction necessarily exists between Biblical creation theology and scientific cosmology. The scientific and theological questions are understood as operating on different levels: science addresses mechanism, theology addresses meaning and ultimate cause.

sections.title: Scholarly Perspectives and Caution Against Overclaiming
sections.body:
Both Islamic and Christian scholarly traditions have developed sophisticated approaches to comparing scripture with science, emphasizing the importance of precision and avoiding false parallels.

Islamic scholars distinguish between revelation (wahy), which is infallible in what it intends to convey, and human understanding of nature (tafsir). The Quran's primary purpose is guidance in belief and practice; scientific accuracy, while not incompatible with revelation, is not its primary function. Prominent figures like Mehdi Golshani and Muzaffar Iqbal have argued that while Islamic cosmological language is compatible with modern physics, the Quran should not be read as a physics textbook or pressed to yield specific scientific predictions.

Christian scholars such as John Polkinghorne (a theoretical physicist and theologian) have similarly cautioned against what he calls 'concordism'—the attempt to force Biblical language into precise agreement with current science. Such attempts are vulnerable to criticism when science updates; they also risk reducing theology to a footnote on science. The mainstream Christian theological position is that creation is real, divinely ordered, and that humanity's scientific understanding of its mechanisms is a valid pursuit that does not displace theological understanding.

The broader scholarly consensus across both traditions holds that:

1. Scripture and science operate in different registers. Scripture makes theological claims about ultimate meaning and purpose; science describes natural mechanisms.

2. Compatibility is possible but requires care. One can affirm both Big Bang cosmology and belief in divine creation, but not by simply equating them.

3. Ancient texts written in ancient cosmological language cannot be reliably mined for specific predictions of modern physics without risking anachronism.

4. Genuine dialogue between scripture and science is enriching, but it requires humility from both sides—from theologians about what texts can be asked to prove, and from scientists about the limits of naturalistic explanation in addressing questions of ultimate meaning.

Muslim audiences have sometimes encountered popular literature claiming explicit Quranic prediction of the Big Bang, cosmic expansion, or other modern physics. While such works express genuine intellectual enthusiasm, scientific specialists and exegetical scholars tend to view these claims as overstated.

A more substantial secular skepticism, however, points out a methodological problem: the same interpretive strategy used to find 'hints' of the Big Bang in Quran 21:30 (retroactive alignment of ancient language with modern theory) would, if applied consistently to other Quranic verses, also have to 'confirm' cosmological ideas now known to be wrong—creating an unfalsifiable system. Critics point to specific verses to give this objection teeth: Quran 18:86 describes Dhul-Qarnayn reaching the setting of the sun and finding it "[as if] setting in a spring of dark mud" (Arabic: ʿaynin ḥami'ah)—language critics argue, read with the same 'literal modern-science' method applied to 21:30, would describe the sun physically descending into a terrestrial body of water or mud rather than being 93 million miles away. Similarly, Quran 67:5 states that the stars adorning the nearest heaven are "what is thrown at the devils" (Arabic: rujūman lil-shayāṭīn)—language critics read as placing stars within the earth's atmosphere, rather than at the vast interstellar distances established by modern astronomy. Mainstream Islamic exegesis responds to both: on 18:86, classical and modern commentators (including At-Tabari and Ibn Kathir) read 'wajadaha' ('he found it') as phenomenological language reporting how the sunset appeared to Dhul-Qarnayn from his vantage point at the western coast he had reached, not a divine assertion about the sun's actual physical location; on 67:5, commentators distinguish the 'rujum' (things thrown, understood as meteors and shooting phenomena) from the fixed stars themselves, which Quran 37:6 separately describes as merely adorning the 'nearest heaven' in appearance, not in physical proximity. Critics counter that this phenomenological reading is applied selectively—invoked to explain away apparent errors but set aside when the same 'nearest heaven' and 'joined then separated' language is instead read literally to claim scientific precision in 21:30 or 51:47. This is, at its core, a dispute about interpretive consistency rather than about whether faith and physics can coexist. This apologetic genre of Quran-science harmonization traces its modern form to Maurice Bucaille's 1976 work 'The Bible, the Quran and Science,' which scholars including Muslim critics have examined for selection bias, anachronistic reading, and the tendency to ignore verses that do not fit the thesis. The concern is not that faith and physics are incompatible, but that this particular genre of interpretation is methodologically unreliable. Mainstream Islamic scholarship, while affirming compatibility between faith and science, has not endorsed the stronger claim that the Quran contains encoded scientific predictions; the more cautious scholarly consensus treats theological affirmation of creation as distinct from scientific discovery of mechanisms.

sections.title: Creation Timescales: Religious and Scientific
sections.body:
An important divergence between ancient religious texts and modern cosmology concerns timescale. The Biblical genealogies have been used historically to calculate the age of creation; Archbishop Ussher's 17th-century calculation placed creation at 4004 BCE, and this approach represents a strand of interpretation that persists in contemporary Christianity. Young Earth Creationism (YEC), which holds that the Earth and universe are approximately 6,000–10,000 years old based on genealogical calculations and a literal reading of the creation days as 24-hour periods, remains a significant minority view within contemporary Christianity. YEC proponents argue that the Hebrew word 'yom' (day) in Genesis 1 refers to literal, sequential 24-hour days, and they interpret the genealogies of Genesis 5 and 11 as providing a reliable chronological framework when read literally. However, modern geology and astronomy establish that the Earth is approximately 4.5 billion years old and the universe is approximately 13.8 billion years old, findings that are incompatible with YEC timescales.

The mainstream of contemporary Christian scholarship and the scientific consensus treat these young-earth interpretations as incorrect, while recognizing YEC as a real, currently-held theological position within Christian communities rather than merely a historical curiosity. Some mainstream Christian scholars attempt to reconcile the creation narrative with modern cosmology through theistic evolution, day-age theory (interpreting the creation days as long geological epochs), or the gap theory (positing an unspecified age between Genesis 1:1 and 1:2). Others treat the cosmological details as secondary to the theological point: that creation is divinely willed, ordered, and meaningful.

The Quranic text does not provide explicit historical chronology in the same way. The timeframe given in Surah 41:12—that the heavens were completed in two days—is subject to interpretive debate. Classical scholars understood these 'days' as possibly referring to periods beyond literal 24-hour days (yawm as a general period of time), while modern readers sometimes interpret them within the context of cosmic time or divine action outside ordinary time.

Mainstream Islamic scholarship, like mainstream Christian scholarship, accepts the scientific establishment of the universe's age through physics and astronomy as the accurate description of physical reality. Creation narratives are understood primarily in theological rather than chronological terms. Some scholars argue that the Quranic and Biblical purpose is not to provide a physics timeline but to assert that creation is not eternal, not accidental, and not meaningless—that it is the product of divine will. This position accommodates modern cosmology while maintaining theological affirmation of divine creation.

sections.title: Points of Convergence and Genuine Theological Significance
sections.body:
Despite the differences in approach, several points of genuine convergence exist between Islamic and Biblical cosmologies when viewed through a theological lens:

First, both traditions affirm that the universe is not eternal. The Quran repeatedly emphasizes that God alone is without beginning (Qadim), while creation came into being through divine will. Similarly, Christian theology from Augustine onward has rejected the notion of an eternal universe, holding that creation ex nihilo (from nothing) is the foundation of Christian metaphysics. This theological position aligns with modern cosmology's finding that the universe had a beginning.

Second, both traditions present creation as an ordered, intelligible cosmos responsive to divine will and purpose. The Quranic notion that heavens and earth answered God's call ('We have come willingly') expresses the concept of cosmic order and obedience to natural law. The Biblical presentation of creation as 'good' and as following an ordered sequence similarly expresses confidence in cosmic rationality. Modern science's ability to describe the universe mathematically and to discover consistent natural laws can be theologically understood as reflecting this divinely established order.

Third, both traditions associate creation with manifestation of divine power and wisdom. The Quran emphasizes God's strength in constructing the heavens (bina'aha bi-ayyid). The Psalms declare that creation reveals God's glory. This theological theme—that the universe testifies to divine majesty—has enabled many believers in both traditions to find the discoveries of modern cosmology spiritually enriching rather than threatening.

Fourth, both traditions include the notion of cosmic expansion or extension. While the Quranic 'musi'un' and Biblical 'expanse' (Hebrew raqia) were not initially understood in terms of modern expansion physics, they carry implications of active, ongoing divine relationship with creation. This has allowed for meaningful, if carefully qualified, engagement with modern physics.

What neither Islamic nor Christian tradition does is reduce cosmology to physics, or equate the scientific model with the theological account. The universe's physical mechanism—how it began and evolved—is one question; its ultimate meaning, purpose, and divine origin are another. Both can be true; both questions are worth asking; but they are distinct.

sections.title: Conclusion
sections.body:
Islamic and Biblical cosmologies emerged from theological frameworks centered on divine creation and cosmic order. Modern scientific cosmology has revealed an ancient universe that expanded from an initial singularity and continues to evolve according to physical laws. The relationship between these perspectives is neither one of contradiction nor of simple equivalence.

Scholars in both traditions have found ways to affirm both faith in God as creator and confidence in scientific understanding of natural mechanisms. This requires intellectual humility: neither scripture nor science should be overread. Ancient texts addressing theological questions should not be pressed into service as physics predictions; modern physics, successful as it is in describing mechanisms, does not resolve questions about ultimate meaning, purpose, or existence.

For educated Muslims and Christians engaging with modern cosmology, the appropriate stance is one of appreciation: appreciation for the theological depth of their traditions' creation narratives, appreciation for the genuine knowledge achieved by scientific inquiry, and appreciation for the genuine difference between these enterprises. The universe's vastness and age, as revealed by modern astronomy, can deepen rather than diminish the sense that creation manifests divine power, wisdom, and purpose.

Further engagement with this topic requires reading scripture in its own terms, understanding science as an ongoing conversation with evidence rather than a fixed doctrine, and resisting the temptation to make one realm of discourse simply collapse into another. In that spirit of intellectual precision and mutual respect, both believers and scientists can authentically explore the origins and nature of creation.

quranVerses.arabic: أَوَلَمْ يَرَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱلسَّمَٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ كَانَتَا رَتْقًۭا فَفَتَقْنَٰهُمَا ۖ وَجَعَلْنَا مِنَ ٱلْمَآءِ كُلَّ شَىْءٍ حَىٍّ ۖ أَفَلَا يُؤْمِنُونَ
quranVerses.translation: Have those who disbelieved not considered that the heavens and the earth were a joined entity, and We separated them and made from water every living thing? Then will they not believe?
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.translation: And the heaven We constructed with strength, and indeed, We are [its] expander.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: هُوَ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ لَكُم مَّا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ جَمِيعًۭا ثُمَّ ٱسْتَوَىٰٓ إِلَى ٱلسَّمَآءِ فَسَوَّىٰهُنَّ سَبْعَ سَمَٰوَٰتٍۢ ۚ وَهُوَ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَلِيمٌۭ
quranVerses.translation: It is He who created for you all of that which is on the earth. Then He directed Himself to the heaven, [His being above all creation], and made them seven heavens, and He is Knowing of all things.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: ثُمَّ ٱسْتَوَىٰٓ إِلَى ٱلسَّمَآءِ وَهِىَ دُخَانٌۭ فَقَالَ لَهَا وَلِلْأَرْضِ ٱئْتِيَا طَوْعًا أَوْ كَرْهًۭا قَالَتَآ أَتَيْنَا طَآئِعِينَ
quranVerses.translation: Then He directed Himself to the heaven while it was smoke and said to it and to the earth, "Come [into being], willingly or by compulsion." They said, "We have come willingly."
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: فَقَضَىٰهُنَّ سَبْعَ سَمَٰوَاتٍۢ فِى يَوْمَيْنِ وَأَوْحَىٰ فِى كُلِّ سَمَآءٍ أَمْرَهَا ۚ وَزَيَّنَّا ٱلسَّمَآءَ ٱلدُّنْيَا بِمَصَٰبِيحَ وَحِفْظًۭا ۚ ذَٰلِكَ تَقْدِيرُ ٱلْعَزِيزِ ٱلْعَلِيمِ
quranVerses.translation: And He completed them as seven heavens within two days and inspired in each heaven its command. And We adorned the nearest heaven with lamps and as protection. That is the determination of the Exalted in Might, the Knowing.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: ءَأَنتُمْ أَشَدُّ خَلْقًا أَمِ ٱلسَّمَآءُ ۚ بَنَىٰهَا
quranVerses.translation: Are you a more difficult creation or is the heaven? Allah constructed it.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.translation: He raised its ceiling and proportioned it.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

bibleVerses.text: In the beginning, God created the heavens and the earth.

The earth was formless and empty. Darkness was on the surface of the deep and God’s Spirit was hovering over the surface of the waters. God said, “Let there be light,” and there was light. God saw the light, and saw that it was good. God divided the light from the darkness. God called the light “day”, and the darkness he called “night”. There was evening and there was morning, the first day.

God said, “Let there be lights in the expanse of the sky to divide the day from the night; and let them be for signs to mark seasons, days, and years; and let them be for lights in the expanse of the sky to give light on the earth”; and it was so. God made the two great lights: the greater light to rule the day, and the lesser light to rule the night. He also made the stars. God set them in the expanse of the sky to give light to the earth, and to rule over the day and over the night, and to divide the light from the darkness. God saw that it was good. There was evening and there was morning, a fourth day.
The heavens declare the glory of God. The expanse shows his handiwork.

For by him all things were created, in the heavens and on the earth, things visible and things invisible, whether thrones or dominions or principalities or powers; all things have been created through him, and for him. He is before all things, and in him all things are held together.
By faith, we understand that the universe has been framed by the word of God, so that what is seen has not been made out of things which are visible.

faq.question: Does Quran 21:30 predict the Big Bang theory?
faq.answer: Quran 21:30 describes the heavens and earth as initially joined and then separated. Some Muslim scholars interpret this as compatible with Big Bang cosmology, while others caution against reading specific scientific predictions into theological language. The verse makes a theological point about cosmic origin and divine power rather than describing the mechanism of cosmic expansion. Both positions—that it is compatible with modern physics and that it should not be pressed as a scientific prediction—are represented in mainstream Islamic scholarship.

faq.question: Does the Bible describe cosmic expansion?
faq.answer: The Bible does not explicitly describe cosmic expansion as understood in modern physics. Biblical language about the 'expanse' of the sky and creation's ongoing nature has been examined by Christian scholars for compatibility with modern cosmology, but the Bible was written in the cosmological framework of the ancient Near East. Christian scholarship generally avoids claiming that the Bible predicted modern physics, instead focusing on showing compatibility between creation theology and scientific understanding.

faq.question: What do Islamic and Christian scholars say about the universe's age?
faq.answer: Both Islamic and Christian mainstream scholarship accept the scientific consensus that the universe is approximately 13.8 billion years old and the Earth is approximately 4.5 billion years old. Historical attempts to calculate creation dates from genealogies or textual references are no longer considered reliable for determining cosmic age. Religious texts are understood primarily as conveying theological truths about creation rather than as chronological documents.

faq.question: Can belief in God as creator be compatible with acceptance of Big Bang cosmology?
faq.answer: Yes, according to mainstream scholars in both traditions. The Big Bang describes the physical mechanism by which the universe expanded from an initial state; it does not address the question of ultimate origin or purpose. Many believers in both Islam and Christianity affirm both the scientific accuracy of Big Bang cosmology and their faith that the universe is created by God. These operate at different levels of explanation.

faq.question: What does 'ratq' and 'fatq' in Quran 21:30 specifically mean?
faq.answer: Ratq (رتق) literally means 'joined' or 'fused,' while fatq (فتق) means 'separated' or 'split.' Classical Islamic exegetes interpreted these terms theologically—referring to God's act of bringing order and differentiation to creation. The terms carry the sense of moving from an undifferentiated state to one of cosmic order. Modern interpreters have noted that this language is compatible with cosmic expansion, but the Quranic purpose is theological rather than descriptive of physical mechanism.

faq.question: Is there a scholarly consensus on whether ancient texts predicted modern physics?
faq.answer: Mainstream scholars in both Islamic and Christian traditions caution against claiming that ancient religious texts predicted specific features of modern physics. While compatibility between theology and science is possible and meaningful, ancient texts addressed in their own terms—theological and moral—should not be read primarily as science texts. Both traditions distinguish between the theological reality of creation and the scientific understanding of cosmic mechanisms. Finding resonance between these is enriching, but requires precision and humility about what the texts intend to convey.

furtherReading.title: Islam and Science
furtherReading.author: Muzaffar Iqbal
furtherReading.note: Historical and philosophical analysis of the relationship between Islamic civilization and scientific inquiry, examining the scriptural foundations and cultural conditions that shaped Islamic scientific tradition.

furtherReading.title: Science and Religion in Quest of Truth
furtherReading.author: John Polkinghorne
furtherReading.note: Theoretical physicist and theologian's careful examination of how science and religious belief address different questions, with analysis of common errors in concordism and the proper relationship between faith and empirical discovery. Yale University Press, 2011.

furtherReading.title: The Holy Qur'an and the Sciences of Nature
furtherReading.author: Mehdi Golshani
furtherReading.note: Iranian physicist-philosopher's exploration of how Quranic teachings relate to natural science, with emphasis on proper interpretation of scriptural language and avoidance of anachronistic readings of ancient texts.

furtherReading.title: God's Undertaker: Has Science Buried God?
furtherReading.author: John Lennox
furtherReading.note: Mathematician and philosopher of science's Christian-apologetics response to New Atheism, examining cosmological fine-tuning, the origin of the universe, and the limits of naturalistic explanation; addressed primarily to a Christian audience, with only passing comparative references to other traditions.
